evidence_quotes.quote: disproportionately targeted at minors and vulnerable populations (groups prone to addictive behavior)
Publisher accountability for decommissioning practices, especially in cases where user data and investments are at stake.

paragraphs.text: Thank you for the opportunity to contribute feedback on the Digital Fairness Act. I would like to point out the importance of including video game products and services within the scope of this rule book. The video game industry has exhibited a repeated pattern of consumer rights violations, particularly concerning digital ownership, monetization practices, and the preservation of purchased content. A growing public discussion, including an inquiry such as the UK House of Lords' 2019 report on Immersive and Addictive Technologies (Oral discussion that compares loot boxes to gambling), and academic publications (Daniel L. King, Paul H. Delfabbro - Adolescent Addiction: Epidemiology, Assessment, and Treatment (Second Edition) - Chapter 7 - Video game addiction ), has raised concerns over loot boxes ,digital gambling mechanisms, integrated into games and disproportionately targeted at minors and vulnerable populations (groups prone to addictive behavior), as this groups shown to be the most profitable groups (the video game industry coined this groups whales). While some parts of the industry tries self-regulation, major publishers such as Electronic Arts, Activision Blizzard, 2K Games and Ubisoft continue to utilize these mechanics in high-revenue titles. Beyond monetization, a critical issue lies in digital product preservation. In an era defined by always-online games, live-service games, and free-to-play models, access to purchased or invested content is dependent on the publisher's discretion. When games are deemed no longer financially viable, they may be permanently discontinued, with no legal obligation to preserve user access, even for paying customers. Notable examples include: - Electronic Arts closure of Anthem (2021) after years of live-service development. - Ubisofts deactivation of online services for legacy titles such as Assassins Creed Liberation HD. - The shutting-down of Google Stadia, which rendered numerous titles inaccessible without publisher intervention. These actions render users monetary and time investments irretrievably lost, raising serious questions of ownership, access, and fairness in the video game industry. As such, I respectfully urge the inclusion of strong consumer protection rights within the Digital Fairness Act that address: - Transparency and regulation of monetization models, i.e. loot boxes. - The preservation of access to purchased digital products. - Publisher accountability for decommissioning practices, especially in cases where user data and investments are at stake. Failing to address these issues may not only harm consumers but also lead to regulatory fragmentation across jurisdictions, damage to market integrity, and loss of cultural assets tied to digital games. Thank you for your attention and for considering these concerns as part of the legislative process.
